title: AstraZeneca — Hematology — Synthesis
html:
AZ Hematology Cross-Study Synthesis

Generated: 2026-04-25 Studies analyzed: CLL ATU Q3 2025 (n=121), MCL ATU Q3 2025 (n=50), CAL KPI Digital Tracker (Q1-Q4 2025 + Q1 2026), CLL ATU Revamp v3.1 (Feb 2026) Data sources: ZoomRx deliverables (Luminas LLC) + AstraZeneca/BeOne earnings + FDA announcements

1. Cross-Study Patterns

Pattern 1: Calquence Is Winning on "Platform" But Losing on "Product" — And This Gap Is Widening

CLL ATU (Q3 2025): Brukinsa leads Calquence on every single-agent perception metric — best in class, most efficacious, safest, best tolerated. The forced choice data is unambiguous: physicians see Brukinsa as the better standalone BTKi.

Same CLL ATU: Calquence leads on combination partner, flexibility, combo options, duration of treatment, and affordability. When weighted by patient volume, Calquence narrows the gap or overtakes Brukinsa in 1L preference.

MCL ATU (Q3 2025): Same pattern in MCL — Calquence is preferred in 1L (48%) where it has the ECHO combination data, but trails Brukinsa in R/R BTKi-naive and trails pirtobrutinib in R/R BTKi-experienced.

Digital Tracker (Q1 2026): Calquence's top-recalled messages are dominated by AMPLIFY (fixed-duration combo) and long-term OS (6-year ELEVATE data). The messaging itself has already pivoted toward "combination platform." The question is whether physician perception follows.

What this means: AZ's entire commercial strategy for Calquence depends on physicians valuing the "platform" attributes (combo, flexibility, duration) as much as or more than "product" attributes (efficacy, safety, tolerability). Right now, product attributes drive prescribing decisions more than platform attributes. The AMPLIFY launch is the moment to test whether the platform narrative can shift preference.

Pattern 2: The "Not Sure" Physician Segment Is the Battleground

CLL ATU Forced Choice (Q3 2025): On key differentiation metrics, 25-31% of HCPs select "Not Sure" between Calquence and Brukinsa:

  • PFS: 31% not sure
  • Afib risk: 30% not sure
  • CV risk: 31% not sure
  • HTN risk: 25% not sure

Trend analysis: "Not sure" rates on PFS have been climbing (19% in Q1 2024 to 31% in Q3 2025). This is NOT because physicians are becoming less informed — it's because Calquence's MAIC parity messaging (CAL23: "No difference in PFS between Calquence and zanubrutinib") is working. Physicians who previously gave Brukinsa the edge are now unsure.

Digital Tracker (Q4 2025): CAL23 (MAIC parity message) has moderate recall (20%) but moderate effectiveness. The direct competitive messages (CAL26: lower HTN and hemorrhage vs zanu; CAL31: similar afib risk vs zanu) are gaining traction.

What this means: Calquence's competitive messaging is successfully creating doubt about Brukinsa's superiority — but it's moving physicians from "Brukinsa preferred" to "Not sure," not from "Brukinsa preferred" to "Calquence preferred." The next step requires converting "not sure" to "Calquence preferred" — and AMPLIFY data (combination efficacy, not just parity on monotherapy) is the tool to do it.

Pattern 3: Physician Adoption of FD Is Real But Slower Than Expected

CLL ATU (Q3 2025): Comfort using BTKi+BCL2i upfront rose to 60% (from 51% in Q4 2024). Likelihood to prescribe post-FDA approval reached 51% (from 42%). These are positive trends.

CAL KPI/ATU (Mar 2026, post-approval): Among physicians using or planning A+V, top drivers are efficacy (68%), treatment duration/FD preference (53%), and safety (38%). But top barriers include OS data immaturity, tolerability concerns (discontinuation, GI), and lack of personal experience.

Treatment sequencing data (Mar 2026): In standard-risk fit patients, 82% still start with covalent BTKi (TTP) in 1L. Only 46% use BTKi+BCL2i (FD) combo in any line. For high-risk patients, only ~5% consistently prefer FD.

MCL ATU (Q3 2025): ECHO adoption showing similar pattern — likelihood to prescribe stable at 58% (T2B), but fewer physicians report already using it vs earlier quarters (despite more saying "within 3 months").

What this means: Physicians like the IDEA of fixed-duration treatment more than they've adopted it in practice. The gap between stated intent (~51% likely to prescribe) and actual current behavior (<25% consistently using FD) is large. This intent-behavior gap is the central challenge for AMPLIFY launch tracking — and the reason AZ is redesigning the ATU to focus on 1L actionability.

Pattern 4: BeOne's Manufacturer Perception Is Improving Faster Than Its Product Perception

CLL ATU (Q3 2025): AZ retains lead on most manufacturer metrics: robustness of studies, balanced data communication, partnership. But BeiGene/BeOne significantly improved on commitment to hematology, commitment to CLL, and innovative hematology pipeline.

MCL ATU (Q3 2025): More dramatic shift — BeOne moved within top 3 on several metrics, surpassing AZ on some (pipeline, commitment to MCL/hematology). The ATU team flagged this as a potential warning signal.

Digital Tracker (Q4 2025): Brukinsa rep reach was volatile (jumped to 60% H/M priority MDs in Q4, fell in Q1 2026). But Brukinsa's messaging is increasingly direct — BRU15 (MAIC improved PFS and CR vs Calquence) is an explicit competitive attack.

What this means: BeOne is executing a deliberate long-game: build manufacturer credibility alongside product promotion. This is exactly what J&J did with DARZALEX in MM — establish the company brand first, then the product portfolio follows. The implication for Calquence is that AZ cannot rely solely on product data to win; they need to match BeOne's investment in manufacturer-level relationship building, especially in CLL where BeOne's commitment messaging is resonating.

Pattern 5: MCL and CLL Show Divergent Competitive Dynamics

CLL: Two-player market (Calquence vs Brukinsa) in 1L, with pirtobrutinib emerging in later lines. Venetoclax-based combos are the "third competitor" in the form of treatment class (FD vs TTP).

MCL: Three-player market (Calquence vs Brukinsa vs pirtobrutinib) with distinct line-of-therapy segmentation. Calquence strongest in 1L (ECHO advantage), Brukinsa in R/R BTKi-naive, pirtobrutinib in R/R BTKi-experienced.

Digital Tracker: Separate message recall tracked for CLL and MCL. In MCL, pirtobrutinib messaging is capturing attention; in CLL, Brukinsa messaging is the primary competitive threat.

What this means: ZoomRx needs to track these as fundamentally different competitive dynamics. A combined "Calquence tracker" masks the distinct challenges. In CLL, the battle is Calquence vs Brukinsa with AMPLIFY as the differentiator. In MCL, the battle is preserving ECHO's 1L advantage while ceding later lines to competitors. The ATU revamp addresses CLL specifically; MCL should get its own strategic review.

2. Measurement Gaps & Opportunities

Gap 1: No Post-Approval AMPLIFY Launch Tracker

The CLL ATU has pre-launch AMPLIFY KPIs, but there is no dedicated post-launch tracker for A+V adoption. The ATU revamp will partially address this, but the frequency (quarterly full reports, mid-quarter KPI views) may not be fast enough for AZ's launch team. Opportunity: Propose a dedicated A+V launch monitor with higher frequency (monthly) and smaller n-sizes focused on A+V prescribers vs non-prescribers.

Gap 2: Community vs Academic Segmentation Missing

AZ earnings cite community expansion as a key growth driver, but neither the CLL ATU nor the MCL ATU provides community vs academic segmentation on key metrics. The sample skews ~70% community, but forced choice and preference data aren't broken out. Opportunity: Add community vs academic cuts to ATU analysis; propose a community-focused sub-study.

Gap 3: A+V vs V+O Switching Dynamics Not Yet Tracked

The revamped ATU adds A+V vs V+O forced choice, but there's no data yet on physicians who currently prescribe V+O and whether they intend to switch to A+V. Understanding the V+O-to-A+V switching rate is critical for estimating AMPLIFY's addressable market. Opportunity: Include a V+O user sub-segment analysis in the revamped ATU or propose it as an ad-hoc study.

Gap 4: Sonrotoclax Awareness Not Tracked

Given BeOne's sonrotoclax pipeline (CELESTIAL-TNCLL in 1L CLL, R/R MCL), there is no current awareness tracking of this next-gen BCL-2i. By the time it enters Phase 3 readouts, it will be too late to establish a baseline. Opportunity: Add sonrotoclax trial awareness items to the CLL ATU trial familiarity section (already being revised in the revamp).

Gap 5: No Competitive Intelligence on BeOne Commercial Tactics

The Digital Tracker captures Brukinsa rep reach and message recall, but doesn't systematically track BeOne's commercial strategy (pricing, access programs, medical education investments, congress presence). Opportunity: Propose a competitive intelligence module or ad-hoc study focused on BeOne's commercial playbook.

3. Data Integrity Notes

  • CLL ATU Q3 2025: n=121 (70% community, 69% target-list) — adequate sample for total-level analysis but sub-group cuts (e.g., by practice type, by region) should be interpreted with caution
  • MCL ATU Q3 2025: n=50 (78% community, 42% target-list) — small sample, reduced from ~100 in Q1. Quarter-to-quarter fluctuations may reflect sampling volatility, not real market shifts. The team reduced frequency from bi-quarterly to quarterly in H2 2025
  • Digital Tracker: n=30 per CLL wave for message recall (n=20 for effectiveness) — low sample sizes, trends directional only
  • ATU revamp data (Mar 2026): n=51 — first wave of redesigned survey; treat as pilot baseline
  • All stated share data is physician-reported, not claims-based — systematically overstates Calquence share relative to Brukinsa (anchoring bias toward familiar brand)
